_meta.course: PROSPER XAU H1 Asian Range Breakout Close
_meta.use: Build-time narration pack for OpenAI, Gemini, Kokoro, or Edge TTS. Do not expose API keys in course_xau.html.
_meta.voice: Warm, calm British English trading tutor. Human, practical, not salesy.
_meta.safety: Study only. Do not create new trading rules or override marker, checklist, journal gate, or backtest contract. Marker v2.2 visuals are display aids only.

xau_t01_trade_definition:
Start here. There is only one funded-path setup in this course: XAU/USD, one-hour chart, clean Asian range, close outside the range, then an execution plan after the close. A wick is not enough. A retest is not enough. DXY is context only. Say it back slowly: one box, one breakout close, one planned risk.

Teach-back prompt: Tell me, out loud: what exact event turns a watch day into a possible trade?
xau_t02_asian_box:
Before you think direction, you draw the box. The tested box is the H1 candles closing midnight to six AM UK time, which means TradingView session twenty-three hundred to zero six hundred because sessions use bar opens. High, low, and midline. Do not predict. The box is the day structure.

Teach-back prompt: What time window builds the tested Asian box, and why does TradingView use 2300-0600?
xau_t03_clean_range:
Now judge the quality by memory and by sight. The tested model has three range gates. No more than eight high or low updates. The biggest Asian candle cannot be more than zero point six five of the range. The whole box must sit between zero point three and three ATR. Visually, beware the box that keeps expanding, or the one giant candle that built most of the range. If any gate fails, no trade.

Teach-back prompt: What visual signs warn you the range may be messy?
xau_t04_breakout_close:
The close is the signal. During the hour, price can poke outside the range and come back in. That is noise. Train your eyes for the candle body closing outside after the H1 candle finishes. A long wick that closes back inside is rejection, not a signal. You wait for the one-hour candle to close outside the box with the buffer, during the eight to nineteen UK signal window. Daily SMA bias and H1 EMA stack must both agree. Only then do you plan the next candle or broker order.

Teach-back prompt: What is the visual difference between a wick-only rejection and a real breakout close?
xau_t05_skip_conditions:
Skipping is part of the system. Messy range, four-hour red-news lockout, rollover danger, shock cooldown if you deliberately enable it, or a work call that stops you checking properly. All of those can block action. Your job is not to catch every move. Your job is to only act when the rule is visible.

Teach-back prompt: Name three reasons you would skip even if price is moving.
xau_t06_risk_prop_rules:
Risk is where beginners turn a good strategy into a bad account. Start tiny. A quarter percent per trade. Stop goes on the opposite side of the Asian box. Target is two R. The marker now draws a red stop zone and green target zone with prices printed inside them. The right edge is the eighteen-hour time exit. Daily stop is two R or less. If the first trade loses, you do not earn a second trade by feeling annoyed.

Teach-back prompt: For a long breakout, what do the red zone, green zone, and right edge mean?
xau_t07_journal_review:
The journal is not admin. It is proof. Record marker state, Action row, range quality, news, screenshot, zone prices, decision, R result, emotion, and one lesson. A clean skip counts. A winner that broke rules is not a good trade. Past marker signals stay on the chart, so scroll back, journal one visible setup, and grade it against the frozen checklist.

Teach-back prompt: Why is a green TP zone not permission to enter by itself?
xau_t08_readiness_gate:
Do not buy funded accounts because you feel ready. Use evidence. Sixty days of study and demo proof. At least thirty journalled forward signals at half or more of model expectancy. Ten marker QA sessions. Course assessment complete. No rule break in the latest ten decisions. Then, and only then, you can talk about funded risk.

Teach-back prompt: What evidence must exist before a funded attempt is allowed?
